title: Terraform Fundamentals: From Infrastructure as Code to Real Deployments
excerpt: Learn Terraform from the ground up: Infrastructure as Code, declarative vs imperative models, core components, workflow, and real-world deployment patterns with AWS.

body.value:
Infrastructure as Code (IaC) is the practice of provisioning and managing infrastructure using code instead of manual processes. The goal is to create environments that are consistent, repeatable, and predictable.

Instead of clicking through cloud consoles, you define your infrastructure in code and let tools handle the provisioning.

value.text: IaC ensures that every deployment produces the same result, eliminating configuration drift and manual errors.

body.value:
There are two main approaches to defining infrastructure:

  • Imperative – you define step-by-step instructions
  • Declarative – you define the desired end state

Terraform uses the declarative model.

value.diagram:
flowchart LR
A[Imperative: HOW] --> B[Step-by-step instructions]
C[Declarative: WHAT] --> D[Desired final state]

body.value: In declarative systems, you describe what you want, and the tool figures out how to achieve it.

Terraform ensures idempotence, meaning applying the same configuration multiple times produces the same result.

If the desired state matches the current state, Terraform does nothing.

Terraform is a tool created by HashiCorp to automate infrastructure provisioning and lifecycle management. It works with cloud providers like AWS, Azure, and many others through APIs.

Terraform is platform-agnostic and operates using a declarative configuration language called HCL (HashiCorp Configuration Language).

value.diagram:
flowchart TD
A["Terraform CLI"] --> B["Configuration Files (.tf)"]
A --> C["Provider Plugins"]
A --> D["State File"]

body.value:
• Terraform Binary – the CLI tool
  • Configuration Files – written in HCL
  • Providers – plugins to interact with APIs
  • State – tracks real infrastructure

value.diagram:
flowchart LR
A[Write Code] --> B[terraform plan]
B --> C[Execution Plan]
C --> D[terraform apply]
D --> E[Infrastructure Created]

body.value:
Terraform follows a predictable workflow:

  • Write – define infrastructure in code
  • Plan – preview changes
  • Apply – execute changes
Terraform stores state in a JSON file that maps your configuration to real-world infrastructure. This allows Terraform to detect changes and apply updates safely.

value.diagram:
flowchart TD
A[Configuration] --> B[Terraform State]
B --> C[Real Infrastructure]
A -->|Compare| C

value.code:
terraform init
terraform plan
terraform apply

body.value: init prepares the environment, plan previews changes, and apply executes them.
Variables allow dynamic configurations, while outputs expose useful values after deployment.

value.text: Never store credentials in code. Use environment variables or secure credential stores.

body.value:
• Use version control (Git)
  • Use variables instead of hardcoding
  • Store state remotely (e.g., S3)
  • Always review plans before applying
This command removes all infrastructure managed by Terraform. Use with caution.
